summary: Three-gun slice now uses yaw 0, right-shifted viewmodels, manifest-derived wall clearance, wall collision feedback, cache-busted public asset URLs, and shared shot-effect pose math.
observations: SPARK is a fast sidearm using blaster-a.glb.
BORE is a close scatter weapon using blaster-h.glb.
VAULT is a heavy pulse weapon using blaster-p.glb.
Each weapon has a procedural Web Audio firing profile until ElevenLabs audio is added.
All three viewmodels use Y rotation 0 after the user requested the guns face the other way.
Shots raycast against the tile level and report lastShot wall hit details through debug state.
Weapon preview/model paths use runtime assetBuild cache invalidation through the manifest helper.
SPARK, BORE, and VAULT view offsets moved to x 0.52, 0.56, and 0.60.
Weapon movement collision footprint is derived from WEAPON_DEFINITIONS through weaponClearance.ts.
WeaponSystem retracts and lowers the viewmodel when the forward ray or weapon probe nears a wall.
Shot tracer origin now uses the active weapon right-side offset instead of a near-center hardcoded start.
Added weaponViewPose.ts so muzzle flash, tracer start, tracer endpoint, and wall impact positions are derived from one camera-local pose helper.
The shared pose helper accounts for weapon view offset, scale, rotation, recoil, and wall-avoidance retraction.
WeaponSystemSnapshot.effectPose exposes rounded shot-effect positions for QA automation.
decisions: Expose the three weapons through a clickable landscape selector.
Use one fire button for this slice.
Leave upgrades, reload button, enemy/projectile behavior, and final hit impact assets for later iterations.
Use primitive tracer and wall impact only as temporary gameplay feedback.
Keep yaw 0 until camera/game-feel review proves a better first-person framing.
Keep collision clearance tied to manifest view offsets to avoid future drift.
Route future muzzle flash, tracer, projectile, and impact placement through getWeaponShotEffectPositions.
caughtIssues: Current assets are prop-scale GLB models, not dedicated animated first-person weapon rigs.
Wall hit feedback currently uses code primitives rather than external assets.
Wall avoidance uses a simplified probe and ray rather than final mesh collision.
Current muzzle flash, tracer, and wall impact visuals are procedural placeholders until external VFX assets are sourced.
risks: Model offsets, right-shifted placement, and yaw 0 presentation may need per-device adjustment once gameplay camera tuning starts.
